title: The Economic Outlook and Monetary Policy
summary: Musalem supported holding the policy rate at 3.5%-3.75%, judging the real rate to be in the lower portion of the neutral range and likely appropriate for some time given a highly uncertain outlook. He sees two-sided risks but leans hawkish, perceiving upside inflation risk (core PCE at 3.1%, sticky core services, tariff and new energy-price pass-through) and warning against 'looking through' supply shocks when underlying inflation is already persistently above target, invoking the 1970s. He views labor risks as weighted to the downside but stresses the unemployment rate remains near its natural rate. He frames his bias symmetrically: he could ease if labor weakens (with inflation contained) but could hike if inflation or inflation expectations move persistently away from 2%, and argues a credible commitment to price stability is itself the pro-growth policy.

key_quotes.quote: I perceive upside risk to inflation—at least of extended persistence of inflation above target.
key_quotes.context: Opening of the Inflation section, with core PCE at 3.1% and sticky core services ex-housing.

key_quotes.quote: I supported the FOMC's recent decision to hold the policy rate in the range of 3.5% to 3.75%.
key_quotes.context: Early statement of his stance; he expects the current rate to remain appropriate for some time.

key_quotes.quote: In real terms, the policy rate appears to be in the lower portion of the neutral range, which I believe is appropriate given the economic outlook, balance of risks and unusual degree of uncertainty.
key_quotes.context: Monetary Policy section, his read on how restrictive the current stance is.

key_quotes.quote: But I believe it would be risky to ease monetary policy solely on the prospect of a future increase in productivity growth, especially with demand pressures at play and inflation running persistently above target today.
key_quotes.context: Closing argument against an AI/productivity-driven case for cuts; defines pro-growth policy as price-stability commitment.
